title: Dog Constantly Licking a Joint: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog licking their joints? Learn about common causes, urgency levels, and how to help your pet find relief. Low urgency triage guide.
content_html:
TL;DR: Joint licking is a sign of underlying pain or irritation that typically requires a non-emergency veterinary visit. Seek immediate care if your dog cannot bear weight on the limb or if there is an open wound.

Why is my dog licking their joints?

  • Communicates discomfort, pain, or inflammation in the underlying tissue or skin.
  • Serves as a self-soothing mechanism for chronic issues like arthritis.
  • Can lead to secondary complications like skin infections or lick granulomas if not addressed.

Is it an emergency if my dog is licking their joint?

  • The urgency level is generally low for chronic issues like osteoarthritis, minor sprains, or allergies.
  • Schedule a regular appointment with your veterinarian to address the underlying cause.
  • Seek care more promptly if your dog is completely unable to put weight on the leg.
  • Seek immediate attention if there is a large, open wound.

How can taking a photo of my dog's joint help the veterinarian?

  • Provides a clear, high-resolution record of the joint and surrounding skin for triage.
  • Allows the vet to see the area exactly as it appeared at home, unaffected by clinic-induced adrenaline.
  • Helps identify redness, swelling, or hair loss that may be obscured during a physical exam or change over time.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Constant licking of a joint in dogs may stem from various underlying causes, including joint trauma leading to luxation, subluxation, fracture, or instability, and potentially septic arthritis due to direct penetration of the joint capsule. Chronic inflammation from immune-mediated diseases or idiopathic polyarthritis, particularly in predisposed breeds such as German Shepherds, Doberman Pinschers, retrievers, spaniels, and pointers, can also contribute. Cranial cruciate ligament rupture, often due to trauma or degeneration, is another common cause, potentially accompanied by plasmacytic-lymphocytic synovitis. Damage to intra-articular ligaments and menisci can further destabilize the joint and exacerbate the condition.

Chapter: Orthopedics, Immunology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1198)

faqs.question: Can I use a cone to stop the licking?
faqs.answer: Yes, using an Elizabethan collar (cone) is a safe way to prevent your dog from causing further damage to the skin while you wait for your veterinary appointment.

faqs.question: Should I apply any creams to the joint?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid over-the-counter creams until you speak with a vet, as many human medications are toxic to dogs if ingested during licking.

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of joint licking?
faqs.answer: The most common causes include arthritis pain, skin allergies, small foreign objects (like thorns), or boredom-induced behavioral habits.
